one_line_takeaway: By reformulating backdoor/spurious-correlation correction as rank-one model editing with an attribution-guided, dynamically-chosen edit layer, the method removes backdoor triggers (e.g., ASR 99.94%->1.34% on CIFAR-10) and reduces spurious-feature reliance using as few as one cleansed sample, without the large-scale retraining or accuracy loss of prior patching/fine-tuning baselines.
key_finding: On backdoor rectification with only n=1 cleansed sample, Dynamic rectifying reduces attack success rate (ASR) to 1.34% on CIFAR-10 and 1.61% on ImageNet (from a Trojaned baseline of 99.94%/87.24% ASR respectively) while retaining overall accuracy of 93.65%/66.77%, matching or exceeding methods (P-ClArC, A-ClArC, fine-tuning, neuron patching) that use n=20 samples; with n=20, Dynamic rectifying drives ASR down to 0.26% (CIFAR-10) / 0.12% (ImageNet) at 93.61%/68.84% accuracy, outperforming all baselines on both axes simultaneously. On spurious correlation (Table 4), Dynamic rectifying with n=20 narrows the spurious-vs-clean accuracy gap to +0.12 points on CIFAR-10 (94.30% clean vs 94.42% spurious) and +2.08 on ImageNet, versus a +5.58/+10.41-point gap for the unedited benign model and worse gaps or accuracy collapse for P-ClArC/A-ClArC. On feature leakage (BlockMNIST, Table 5), Dynamic rectifying (n=1) achieves 99.03% accuracy with feature-leakage score 2.417, better on both axes than the benign model (99.17% acc, 3.597 leakage), IG-SUM regularization (94.14% acc, 3.417 leakage), and 20-sample fine-tuning (98.67% acc, 2.929 leakage). On the real-world ISIC skin-lesion task (Table 6, EfficientNet-B4), Dynamic rectifying with n=10 reduces the spurious-accuracy inflation to +1.50 points (61.00% clean vs 62.50% spurious) versus +26.00 for the unedited benign model, at 80.00% overall accuracy. Generalization tables (2 and 3) show a single-sample rectified model trained against one trigger visibility/location still suppresses ASR for triggers of other visibilities (Γ0.3-Γ1.0, all reduced to roughly 5-8% from ~95-99%) and other spatial locations (ΓTL/ΓC/ΓBL reduced to roughly 6-9% from ~99.6-99.9%), substantially better than a pruning-based 'Patched model' baseline (~27-38% ASR range in both tables).
core_contribution: The paper reframes rank-one model editing (previously used for knowledge injection/domain adaptation in generative and discriminative models) as a technique for 'model rectification': correcting a trained model's unreliable behavior on corrupted inputs (neural Trojans/backdoors, spurious correlations, feature leakage) using as few as a single clean/corrupted sample pair, without full retraining. It (1) formalizes this rectification setting and proves two theoretical properties distinguishing it from standard domain-adaptation editing -- 'rectifiability' (the edit key k* lies in-span of the learned key statistics when the susceptible model was trained on both clean and corrupted inputs, so no out-of-span residual degrades other associations) and 'span-aligned control' (paired supervision removes the large-sample requirement that domain-adaptation editing needs under distribution shift); (2) identifies that rank-one editing effectiveness is layer-dependent and no single fixed layer (e.g., the conventional last-layer choice) is universally optimal, then derives an attribution-guided layer-localization method based on Integrated Gradients, remapped into the rank-one editing direction (C^-1 k*), to automatically select the layer offering the largest first-order reduction in the corrupted-vs-clean prediction discrepancy; and (3) integrates this into a 'Dynamic Model Rectifying' algorithm (Algorithm 1) that iteratively locates the suspect layer, applies a rank-one edit, and checks an overall accuracy-degradation budget before accepting the edit.
threat_model: The paper treats three unreliability sources under a unified clean/corrupted-pair formulation, not a single adversarial threat model: (1) Neural Trojans/backdoors -- a subset of training samples is poisoned with a trigger pattern and mislabeled to a target class ~y, producing a model that misclassifies triggered test inputs (attacker controls training-time poisoning; defender/practitioner has white-box access to the trained model plus a small number of cleansed correct-label samples to rectify it). (2) Feature spurious correlation -- class-irrelevant patterns are attached to a portion of class-y training samples, causing the model to rely on the spurious feature even though it still predicts the correct label on those inputs (a non-adversarial, distributional reliability problem, i.e., 'Clever Hans' behavior, not an attack). (3) Feature leakage -- the model incorporates uninformative artifacts (e.g., a null patch region in BlockMNIST) into its decision process. The paper's own framing (Related Work, Sec. 2) explicitly scopes itself to backdoor attacks and spurious correlations as the security-relevant behaviors, while noting adversarial (evasion) attacks are set aside because they require full model access at inference and are out of scope. So only the neural-Trojan setting is a genuine security/attack scenario; spurious correlation and feature leakage are robustness/reliability problems, not attacks, and the paper does not claim otherwise.
limitations: (2) Explicitly out of scope: the paper defers adversarial (inference-time evasion) attacks, citing full-model-access requirements as a practical limitation of that threat model, so no evasion-attack results are reported. (3) All experiments use CNN classifiers (ResNet-18, EfficientNet-B4); the layer-localization method is stated to be inapplicable to token-level causal analysis used in LLM editing work, and the paper explicitly notes LLM-style layer-localization techniques do not transfer to vision classifiers -- the converse (whether the proposed vision-specific method transfers to LLMs) is not tested. (4) Rectification depends on having at least one paired clean/corrupted sample per unreliability instance; the paper reports results with n as low as 1 but does not characterize how rectification degrades or fails when no representative corrupted sample of a given trigger/spurious-feature type is available at rectification time.

datasets_used: BlockMNIST (Shah et al., MNIST variant with null patch)
ISIC skin lesion dataset (Codella et al.)
quantitative_results: Backdoor, CIFAR-10, Dyn. rectifying n=1: OA 93.65%, ASR 1.34% (vs Trojaned model OA 93.67%, ASR 99.94%)
Backdoor, ImageNet, Dyn. rectifying n=1: OA 66.77%, ASR 1.61% (vs Trojaned model OA 69.05%, ASR 87.24%)
Backdoor, CIFAR-10, Dyn. rectifying n=20: OA 93.61%, ASR 0.26%
Backdoor, ImageNet, Dyn. rectifying n=20: OA 68.84%, ASR 0.12%
Spurious correlation, CIFAR-10, Dyn. rectifying n=20: overall 93.99%, clean 94.30%, spurious 94.42% (+0.12 gap) vs benign model +5.58 gap
Spurious correlation, ImageNet, Dyn. rectifying n=20: overall 68.94%, clean 81.25%, spurious 83.33% (+2.08 gap) vs benign model +10.41 gap
Feature leakage, BlockMNIST, Dyn. rectifying n=1: accuracy 99.03%, leakage score 2.417 (vs benign model 99.17% accuracy / 3.597 leakage; IG-SUM 94.14% accuracy / 3.417 leakage)
ISIC skin lesion, Dyn. rectifying n=10 (EfficientNet-B4): overall 80.00%, clean 61.00%, spurious 62.50% (+1.50 gap) vs benign model overall 79.00%, +26.00 gap
Trigger-visibility generalization (Table 2), n=1: Dyn. rectifying OA 91.21%, ASR range Γ0.3-Γ1.0 ≈5.17-7.91% vs Patched model ≈26.86-37.19% and Benign model ≈95.29-99.21%
Trigger-location generalization (Table 3), n=1: Dyn. rectifying OA 90.85%, ASR range ΓBR-ΓBL ≈6.36-9.47% vs Patched model ≈29.31-34.88% and Benign model ≈99.57-99.90%
baselines_compared: P-ClArC (projective class artifact compensation, Anders et al.)
A-ClArC (augmentative class artifact compensation, Anders et al.)
IG-SUM robust attribution regularization (Chen et al., NeurIPS 2019)
Static rectifying (the paper's own last-layer-only ablation variant, used as an internal baseline against the full dynamic method)
